title: Towards canonical bases in homology of symplectic resolutions
speaker: Roman Bezrukavnikov
abstract: I will describe a program aimed at understanding numerics of representations of quantized symplectic resolutions in positive characteristic. A key ingredient is a generalization of the action of the affine braid group on the derived category of coherent sheaves on the Springer resolution. Partly based on a joint project with Okounkov and an earlier paper with Anno and Mirkovic.
tags: Workshop on Representation Theory and Geometry

proof_stages.label: Symplectic-resolution input and H2 parameter space
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the basic geometric setting: a symplectic resolution X -> Y with X modeled on T*G/P or a transversal-slice variant, together with the simplifying identification V = H2(X) = H2(G/P).

proof_stages.label: Hyperplane chambers and marked birational resolutions
proof_stages.summary: Coroot hyperplanes in V_R and their affine analogues organize the different parabolics and associated resolutions X_c, each equipped with a common marking of H2.

proof_stages.label: Dual torus local system of derived categories
proof_stages.summary: Passing to the dual torus and its toric compactification, the speaker formulates a canonical W-equivariant local system of equivariant derived categories whose stalks are Db Coh^{G_m}(X_c) and whose K-theoretic monodromy matches equivariant quantum cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Canonical-basis axioms from flat sections and monodromy
proof_stages.summary: On the universal cover of the complement of the affine hyperplanes, canonical bases of flat K-theory sections over alcoves are characterized up to sign by symmetry, involution/asymptotic orthonormality, and a half-monodromy Gram-Schmidt wall-crossing rule.

proof_stages.label: Existence and representation-theoretic realization
proof_stages.summary: Existence of the bases is traced to earlier work using Frobenius weights on the affine flag variety, and the basis vectors are identified with classes of irreducibles in noncommutative resolutions, including modular-representation categories in the Springer case.

proof_stages.label: Perverse wall-crossing governed by central charge
proof_stages.summary: The wall-crossing equivalences between the abelian categories attached to neighboring alcoves are described as perverse equivalences, with the perversity filtration determined by the order of vanishing of a central-charge polynomial zeta along the crossed face.

proof_stages.label: Stable envelopes and extension to general symplectic resolutions
proof_stages.summary: K-theoretic stable envelopes are shown or conjectured to be transported by the same path equivalences, leading to a broader conjectural framework for arbitrary symplectic resolutions, with quiver-variety results in progress and mirror-dual interpretations.

examples.name: Dual Grassmannians giving the same resolution target
examples.note: The pair Gr(k,n) and Gr(n-k,n) illustrates the point that different parabolics can yield resolutions of the same orbit, motivating the chamber bookkeeping in V.
examples.engagement_reason: He only cites Grassmannian k,n and Grassmannian n−k,n as an example of different parabolics giving resolutions of the same orbit.

passes.reason: standard dual Grassmannians are used to show that the same resolution target need not determine a unique parabolic resolution
Dual Grassmannians are homogeneous Hermitian-symmetric varieties, used here to show non-uniqueness of parabolic resolutions of the same target.
The dual Grassmannians are highly homogeneous GL_n/P varieties and are used to show uniqueness of the parabolic/resolution target fails.
Dual Grassmannians are highly homogeneous symmetric spaces, and here they show that different parabolics can give the same resolution target.
Grassmannians are homogeneous highly symmetric varieties, and the pair is invoked to show that a fixed orbit/target need not determine a unique parabolic resolution.
The dual Grassmannian resolutions are ordinary members of a systematic type-A family, used to show non-uniqueness of the resolution/parabolic for a fixed target.
Dual Grassmannians form a highly homogeneous classical family and are used to show the parabolic/resolution target is not unique.
Used to exhibit non-uniqueness of the parabolic-to-resolution-target correspondence; Grassmannians are a highly homogeneous systematic family.
Used to show non-uniqueness of the parabolic resolution target; Grassmannians are homogeneous symmetric varieties in an infinite family.
The dual Grassmannians Gr(k,n) and Gr(n−k,n) are homogeneous symmetric varieties used to show non-uniqueness of resolutions of the same target.
The dual homogeneous Grassmannians Gr(k,n) and Gr(n−k,n) form a symmetric infinite-family pair and are used to show uniqueness of the resolution target fails.
used to exhibit failure of uniqueness of the resolution target, while nontrivial dual Grassmannians are ordinary members of an infinite family

examples.name: Perverse shifts versus ordinary shifts
examples.note: The familiar relation between perverse and ordinary shifts is invoked only to make the definition of a perverse equivalence concrete before applying it to wall-crossing functors.
examples.engagement_reason: He says perverse equivalence generalizes the relation between perverse shifts and ordinary shifts, but does not work through that relation as a concrete example.

passes.reason: the comparison of perverse and ordinary shifts is a standard motivating prototype for perverse equivalence, not a refuting or structurally special case
The perverse-versus-ordinary shift comparison is a standard motivating model for perverse equivalences, not a structurally special object or a refutation.
The perverse-versus-ordinary shift comparison is a standard derived-categorical model for perverse equivalence, not a structurally special obstruction.
The perverse-versus-ordinary shift relation is a standard motivating comparison for perverse equivalences, not a failure case or structurally special object.
the comparison of perverse and ordinary shifts is a standard motivating case for perverse equivalences, not a sporadic, limiting, extremal, or failure example.
The perverse-versus-ordinary shift comparison is a standard motivating prototype for perverse equivalence, not an adversarial or structurally sporadic case.
The perverse-versus-ordinary shift comparison is a standard motivating model for perverse equivalences, not an adversarial or structurally exceptional object.
The ordinary shift is the collapsed constant-perversity/trivial-filtration case motivating perverse equivalences, not a refutation.
Used as a motivating comparison, with ordinary shifts serving as the trivial/base case of the more stratified perverse-shift behavior.
The perverse-shift versus ordinary-shift comparison is a standard motivating instance for perverse equivalences, with no sporadic or extremal structure singled out.
The perverse-versus-ordinary shift comparison is a standard motivating model for perverse equivalence, with no intrinsic sporadic, extreme, degenerate, or maximal-symmetry feature.
the ordinary-shift comparison is the collapsed/base-case model motivating perverse equivalence, not a refutation

examples.name: Mirror dual family for T*G/B
examples.note: In response to a question, the expected mirror family parameterized by regular semisimple conjugacy classes in the dual group illustrates how the toric/dual-torus picture is related to mirror-dual geometry.
examples.engagement_reason: In the Q&A he describes the expected mirror dual family for T*G/B in terms of regular semisimple conjugacy classes, but only states this identification without carrying out a computation or construction on it.

passes.reason: T*G/B is the complete-flag/Borel endpoint of the T*G/P family and is invoked as a mirror-symmetry instance rather than an obstruction
The mirror family is discussed for the Borel/full-flag case T*G/B, an extremal minimal-parabolic member of the T*G/P Springer-resolution family, not as a counterexample.
T*G/B is the cotangent bundle of the complete flag variety, a highly G-symmetric endpoint of the T*G/P family, used illustratively for mirror duality.
The mirror dual family for T*G/B is cited as part of a systematic mirror-duality picture, not as a sporadic, limiting, or adversarial example.
the mirror family for the Springer resolution T*G/B is used as a positive expected realization in a uniform Lie-theoretic family, not as an obstruction or classification exception.
T*G/B is the full Springer resolution corresponding to the Borel/minimal-parabolic extreme among T*G/P examples, used illustratively for mirror duality.
The mirror family for T*G/B is invoked as a systematic expected mirror, with the Borel/full-flag Springer resolution lying at an extreme of the G/P family.
Used as an illustrative mirror-dual family; T*G/B is the full-flag Springer resolution, an endpoint case among T*G/P resolutions with large G-symmetry.
Used as a conjectural mirror-family instance; T*G/B is the highly homogeneous full-flag Springer resolution at the Borel/most-refined parabolic extreme.
The mirror dual family for T*G/B is cited illustratively; T*G/B is the highly homogeneous Springer resolution at the full-flag endpoint of the G/P family.
The mirror family is tied to T*G/B, the highly homogeneous full-flag Springer resolution at the Borel endpoint of the G/P family, and is cited as a related construction rather than a refutation.
the mirror family is cited as an expected mirror construction, and T*G/B is the full-flag/Borel endpoint of the T*G/P family
